title: REPUBLIC OF KENYA
text:
REPUBLIC OF KENYA
IN THE TAX APPEALS TRIBUNAL AT NAIROBI
TAT APPEAL NO. E316 OF 2025
RIANA PROPERTIES LIMITED...........................................……….…....
……APPELLANT
-VS-
COMMISSIONER OF LEGAL SERVICES & BOARD COORDINATION.....
RESPONDENT
JUDGMENT
BACKGROUND
1. The Appellant is a private limited company registered in Kenya and
whose principal business is running rental services.
2. The Respondent is the principal officer appointed under section 13 of
the Kenya Revenue Authority Act. The Kenya Revenue Authority is an
agency of the Government of Kenya mandated with the duty of
collecting and receipting all tax revenue, and the administration and
enforcement of all tax laws set out in Parts 1 & 2 of the First Schedule
to the Act, for the purposes of assessing, collecting, and accounting for
all tax revenues in accordance with those laws.
3. The Respondent carried out an audit of the Appellant’s tax affairs for
the years of income 2019 to 2023, and issued a Notice of Preliminary
Findings on 19th September 2024.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 1 of
18

4. The Appellant responded to the Respondent’s Notice of Preliminary
Findings on diverse dates, and thereafter, the Respondent issued its
Notice of Assessment on 31st October 2024 demanding Kshs.
117,122,019.00 on account of corporation tax, VAT, and PAYE, inclusive
of penalty and interest.
5. On 5th December 2024, the Appellant filed its Notice of Objection to the
Respondent’s Notice of Assessment.
6. On 31st January 2025, the Respondent issued its Objection Decision,
partially allowing the Appellant’s objection and confirming the
Appellant’s additional tax assessment of Kshs. 73,308,854.00
7. Appellant, being dissatisfied with the Respondent’s Objection Decision,
lodged this Appeal vide Notice of Appeal dated and filed on 14th March
2025.
THE APPEAL
8. In its Memorandum of Appeal dated and filed on 2nd April 2025, the
Appellant raised the following grounds of appeal: -
a. The Respondent erred in law and fact by including non-income
items as taxable, contrary to the provisions of Section 3(2)(a)(iii)
of the Income Tax Act (Cap. 470) of the Laws of Kenya.
b. The Respondent erred in fact and in law by assessing tax based
on the lease agreements and not on the actual amount received
as taxable, contrary to the provisions of Section 6(2) of the
Income Tax Act (Cap. 470) of the Laws of Kenya.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 2 of
18

c. The Respondent erred in fact and in law by arbitrarily disallowing
50% of the operating expenses without providing any legal basis
for such action.
d. The Respondent erred in both fact and law by imposing VAT on
variances identified in its analysis contrary to sections 2 and 5(1)
(a) of the VAT Act (Cap. 476) of the Laws of Kenya.
e. The Respondent erred in fact and law by imposing PAYE on
directors’ remuneration for the periods 2020 and 2022.
APPELLANT’S CASE
9. The Appellant case is based on its Statement of Facts dated and filed
on 2nd April 2025.
10. The Appellant averred that it issued the Respondent a Notice of
Assessment for the periods 2019 to 2023 detailing additional tax
assessments under corporation tax amounting to Kshs. 78,449,927.00,
value-added tax (VAT) amounting to Kshs. 8,489,213.00, and Pay As
You Earn (PAYE) amounting to Kshs. 30, 182,880 inclusive of interest
and penalties.
11. The Appellant contended that the Respondent did not consider
several key factors, including deferred income, advance billings,
exchange rate differences, and non-income items when analyzing the
income reported in the tax returns to reconcile the variances and
discrepancies.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 3 of
18

12. The Appellant stated that a thorough reconciliation of the
declared VAT sales for 2019 to 2023 with the income tax returns
revealed that the “other income” reported in the tax returns – such as
electricity income, signage income, and surrender penalties – was
excluded from the Respondent’s comparison. Specifically: -
a) Electricity income, as stipulated in lease agreements, was
correctly declared in both VAT and income tax returns, indicating
no need for adjustment.
b) Signage income, derived from the license fee payable by
tenants, was similarly reported as taxable income in the VAT
returns. A detailed breakdown of these fees, including samples
from 2019 and 2020 demonstrated that they were in alignment
with the lease agreements. Likewise, penalties for the surrender
of leases by tenants were appropriately recognized as other
income in both the VAT and income tax returns.
c) Deferred income had been treated in accordance with the VAT
Act (Cap. 476) of the Laws of Kenya and International Financial
Reporting Standards (IFRS) for Small and Medium-Sized
Enterprises (SMEs). The VAT Act dictates that rent income should
be taxed at the time of supply, while the income tax return
accounted for income on an accrual basis. The company
accurately recognized income on a straight-line basis, in
compliance with IFRS 16. However, the Respondent’s
assessment failed to incorporate rent reductions during the
COVID-19 pandemic and exchange rate differences, both of
which should have been accounted for.
d) The Respondent’s assessment also incorrectly identified a
variance in 2022, where sales of Kshs. 157,703, 122 were
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 4 of
18

misstated as Kshs. 147,959, 882, resulting in an erroneous
variance in taxable income. The error was rectified.
13. The Appellant further asserted that the Respondent’s analysis
overlooked instances where rent was discounted due to the pandemic
or impacted by exchange rate fluctuations. These variances should not
have been taxed, as they did not constitute actual income in
accordance with Section 6(1) of the Income Tax Act (Cap. 470), which
expresses that rent income should only be taxed on amounts received,
rather than amounts specified in the lease agreements.
14. The Appellant stated that the Respondent also disallowed
operating expenses, claiming insufficient documentary evidence. This
is despite the Appellant submitting ledgers and requesting a sample of
expenses for review, and there being no response from the
Respondent. Nevertheless, the Respondent proceeded to disallow 50%
of the operating expenses without providing a legal basis for this
action. These expenses, being entirely incurred for the purposes of
generating income, ought to be rescinded.
15. On VAT, the Appellant posited that the Respondent proposed to
charge VAT on variances identified as undeclared sales for 2020, 2021
and 2022. The Appellant argued that VAT should only be levied on
actual taxable supplies, not on variances, in accordance with the VAT
Act, which requires that there be a taxable supply for VAT to apply,
and the variances did not meet this criterion.
16. On PAYE, the Appellant posited that the Respondent argued that
the company failed to remit PAYE on directors’ remuneration for 2020
and 2022. The Appellant maintained that full PAYE had been duly
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 5 of
18

deducted and remitted in a timely manner. Additionally, the PAYE for
2020 was calculated at the correct rate of 25%, and not 30% as the
Respondent had stated; as such, the PAYE assessments should be
revoked.
17. It is the Appellant’s case that the Respondent erred in fact and
law by failing to account for deferred income, advance billings,
exchange differences, and non-income items when comparing VAT
returns with income tax (company) returns.
18. The Appellant stated that electricity income is recovered from
tenants as per lease agreements, and while it is categorized as “other
income” in the income tax returns, it is declared as sales in VAT
returns. Supporting invoices confirm that both vatable and exempt
electricity supplies were reported correctly. Similarly, signage income,
representing license fees paid by certain tenants, is included in both
VAT and income tax returns, with agreements and invoices
substantiating these entries.
19. The Appellant stated that another notable item is a surrender
penalty of Kshs. 2,439,050 charged to a tenant for terminating their
lease early. This penalty was declared as “other income” in the income
tax returns and recorded as a taxable sale in the VAT returns for
September 2022.
20. It is the Appellant’s case that deferred income also plays a
significant role in the discrepancies. Some tenants prepay rent
quarterly, leading to timing differences in reporting. VAT regulations
require income to be recognized when invoiced or paid, whereas
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 6 of
18

income tax accounting, in line with IFRS for SMEs, recognizes rent
income on a straight-line basis over the lease term. This results in
temporary differences in income reporting, which subsequently reverse
in future periods.
21. The Appellant stated that the reconciliation demonstrated that
the adjusted VAT sales and gross rent declared in the income tax
returns closely align, with only minor unreconciled variances due to
timing and classification differences. The Respondent’s comparison
overlooked essential factors that influence income recognition, and the
provided lease agreements and invoices substantiate the company’s
tax declarations; consequently, the Respondent ought to rescind the
assessments based on variances, as they do not constitute taxable
income under the law.
22. On income tax, the Appellant asserted that section 6(1) of the
Income Tax Act (cap. 470) defines “gains or profits” from property as
including rent, royalties, premiums, or similar payments received for
its use. Based on this provision, tax on rent income should only be
levied on the actual amounts received, not the figures stated in lease
agreements as undertaken by the Respondent.
23. The Appellant stated that its actual rent collections sometimes
differed from lease agreement amounts due to factors such as rent
discounts granted to tenants during COVID-19 pandemic. The
Respondent, however, failed to consider these reductions, as well as
exchange rate differences, when assessing the tax liability. The same
exchange rate was applied across multiple years, further affecting the
calculations.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 7 of
18

24. The Appellant posited that the Respondent erred in disallowing
related expenses for the period under review. As evidenced by its
correspondence with the Respondent’s team on 2nd October, 2024, the
Appellant provided ledgers, and a request was made for the
Respondent’s team to specify a sample for which the Appellant could
furnish supporting documents. However, the Appellant did not receive
a response regarding the requested sample, and instead, an additional
assessment was issued disallowing 50% of the operating expenses
without providing any legal basis or justification for the percentage
applied.
25. The Appellant stated that given that 50% of the expenses have
been allowed for corporation tax purposes, it is evident that the
Respondent acknowledges that the expenses were incurred in the
production of the income declared within the period under review.
Consequently, the Appellant reiterated that the disallowance of the
remaining 50% is erroneous, as 100% of the expenses were wholly and
exclusively incurred for income generation.
26. Finally, the Appellant asserted that full PAYE was duly deducted
and remitted on the directors’ remuneration by the statutory deadline,
as substantiated by the relevant pay slips.
APPELLANT’S PRAYERS
27. The Appellant prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders: -
a) The Appeal be deemed validly lodged.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 8 of
18

b) The Appeal be allowed, and the Respondent be directed to
rescind the corporation tax, VAT, and PAYE assessments.
c) Any other remedies the Honourable Tribunal deems just and
reasonable.
RESPONDENT'S CASE
28. In opposition to this Appeal, the Respondent filed its Statement
of Facts dated 2nd May 2025, and Written Submissions dated 10th
October, 2025.
29. The Respondent posited that it carried out an analysis of income
declared in the Appellant’s income tax company (IT2C) and VAT
returns, which revealed variances for the years of income 2019 to
2023.
30. The Respondent further posited that it derived income based on
an analysis of the lease agreements availed for the years of income
2020 to 2022, which was compared with income declared in IT2C
returns and revealed undeclared income amounting to Kshs.
46,079,531.00.
31. The Respondent, therefore, adjusted the gross turnover for the
years of income 2020 and 2022 by Kshs. 46,079,531 (higher variance)
and subjected the same to corporation tax.
32. The Respondent averred that it further derived income based on
an analysis of the lease agreements availed for the years 2020 to
2023, and compared it with income declared in VAT returns, which
revealed undeclared income amounting to Kshs. 38,589,557. The
Respondent charged VAT on the variances established.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 9 of
18

33. The Respondent further averred that it carried out a
reconciliation between the Director’s remuneration expensed in the
IT2C returns subjected to PAYE and compared it to PAYE advance
payments, which revealed unpaid PAYE totaling Kshs. 18,804,000 for
the years 2020 and 2022.
34. The Respondent asserted that it noted that some discount
statements were not signed by the tenant/landlord, and therefore
could not confirm whether the discount was granted or not. The
Respondent requested the Appellant to provide a detailed schedule of
the discounts given per tenant, which the Appellant did not provide.
35. It is the Respondent’s case that, owing to gaps, the Respondent
could not ascertain the actual rents received per tenant for the periods
affected by discounts, and therefore the explanations and
reconciliations given failed for both income tax and VAT.
36. The Respondent reiterated its position as stated in the Objection
Decision communicated to the Appellant on 31st January 2025. It is the
Respondent assertion that it has a wide berth in assessing the
correctness or falsity of the said returns as per Section 31 of the Tax
Procedures Act, 2015 (TPA).
37. On the burden of proof, the Respondent posited that Sections 56
of the TPA and 30 of the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act place the burden of
proof on the Appellant, and the Appellant did not provide adequate
documents to fully support its objection.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 10 of
18

38. The Respondent averred that the Appellant summarized expense
ledgers and invoice in support of the expenses, but the ledgers
provided did not have a listing of the specific invoices numbers, hence
making it impossible for the Respondent to tie the numbers provided
to the specific invoices provided.
39. The Respondent further averred that the Appellant ought to keep
records to ensure that the objection meets the conditions set out in the
law on supporting an objection to an assessment.
40. The Respondent reiterated the above averments and
submissions in its Written Submissions filed on 23rd October 2025, and
sought to rely on precedents set in Singapore Motors Limited -v.-
Commissioner of Domestic Taxes (TAT E039 OF 2021) KEHC
2443 (KLR), and Commissioner of Investigations & Enforcement
vs Kidero (TAT E028 of 2020) (2022) KEHC52 (KLR), amongst
others.
RESPONDENT’S PRAYERS
41. The Respondent prayed to the Tribunal for the following orders:
a) The appeal lacks merit and should be dismissed.
b) The Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 31st January 2025
be upheld.
c) Costs be awarded to the Respondent.
ISSUES FOR DETERMINATION
42. The Tribunal, having considered the parties' pleadings,
submissions, and documents filed before it, is of the view that the
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 11 of
18

issue falling for its determination is whether the Respondent’s
Objection Decision dated 31st January, 2025 is justified.
ANALYSIS AND DETERMINATION
43. This Appeal arises from the Respondent’s Objection Decision
dated 31st January 2025, which confirmed additional assessments of
the Appellant of Kshs. 73,308,854.00 on account of corporation tax,
VAT, and PAYE, inclusive of penalty and interest. The Respondent
grounded its decision on the fact that the Appellant failed to support its
objection and provide the documents sought.
44. It is the Respondent’s case that the Appellant provided the
following documents in support of its objection: -
a) Lease agreements.
b) Riana electricity invoices for the year 2023.
c) Audited financial statements.
d) Deferred income ledgers.
e) Deferred income invoices.
f) Operating expenses invoices.
g) Interest expenses invoices.
h) Loan account statements.
i) Mortgage agreements.
j) Bank statements.
k) PAYE payment slips.
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 12 of
18

45. The Respondent submitted that the total net amounts from the
invoices provided in support of the reconciliation did not match the
figures in the reconciliation table and ledgers. Additionally, the ledgers
provided only had totals for each category without a breakdown of the
transactions making up the grand totals.
46. The Respondent reiterated its position as stated in the Objection
Decision communicated to the Appellant on 31st January, 2025. It is the
Respondent assertion that it has a wide berth in assessing the
correctness or falsity of the said returns as per Section 31 of the Tax
Procedures Act, 2015 (TPA).
47. On its part, the Appellant submitted that it sufficiently supported
its objection. It is the Appellant’s case that deferred income also plays
a significant role in the discrepancies, as some tenants prepay rent
quarterly, leading to timing differences in reporting.
48. The Appellant also submitted that the reconciliation
demonstrated that the adjusted VAT sales and gross rent declared in
the income tax returns closely align, with only minor unreconciled
variances due to timing and classification differences. The
Respondent’s comparison overlooked essential factors that influence
income recognition, and the provided lease agreements and invoices
substantiate the company’s tax declarations; consequently, the
Respondent ought to rescind the assessments based on variances, as
they do not constitute taxable income under the law.
49. From the totality of evidence before the Tribunal, it is apparent
that the instant Appeal relates to whether the Appellant supported its
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 13 of
18

objection to explain the discrepancies and variances identified by the
Respondent, and therefore vary the additional assessments.
50. The Tribunal has reviewed documents and records placed before
it by both parties. The documents placed on record by the Appellant
are the same that were also supplied by the Appellant at the objection
stage and itemized in paragraph 44 above, and there is nothing new
that would support the variances and discrepancies identified by the
Respondent in its Objection Decision.
51. The Tribunal reiterates that it is the Appellant’s duty to
sufficiently support its objection. Section 59 of the Tax Procedures Act
provides as follows regarding the production of records: -
(1)For the purposes of obtaining full information in respect of
the tax liability of any person or class of persons, or for
any other purposes relating to a tax law,
the Commissioner or an authorized officer may require
any person, by notice in writing, to –
(a)produce for examination, at such time and place
as may be specified in the notice, any
documents (including in electronic format) that
are in the person's custody or under the person's
control relating to the tax liability of any person;
(b) furnish information relating to the tax liability of
any person in the manner and by the time
specified in the notice; or
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 14 of
18

(c) attend, at the time and place specified in the
notice, for the purpose of giving evidence in
respect of any matter or transaction appearing
to be relevant to the tax liability of any person.
52. On the other hand, Section 56 (1) of the Tax Procedures Act (Cap
469B), which provides the following regarding the burden of proof: -
In any proceedings under this Part, the burden shall
be on the taxpayer to prove that a tax decision is
incorrect.
53. Finally, Section 30 of the Tax Appeals Tribunal Act (cap 469A) is
explicit on burden of proof as follows: -
“30. Burden of proof
In a proceeding before the Tribunal, the appellant
has the burden of proving—(a)where
an appeal relates to an assessment, that the
assessment is excessive; or (b)in any other case,
that the tax decision should not have been made or
should have been made differently.”
54. This position was affirmed by the Honourable Tribunal in its
judgment in TAT No. 435 of 2022 Abyssinia Iron and Steel Ltd -
vs- Commissioner of Customs and Border Control, where it held
thus:
"The bottom line is that once the Appellant has
provided evidence that the Respondent's assessment
was wrong, then the Respondent must push back
and show that its assessment was not arbitrary,
capricious, or imagined. The onus will then shift back
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 15 of
18

to the Appellant once the Respondent has discharged
its burden on a balance of convenience to discharge
the prima facie case that has been presented by the
Respondent.
55. The Tribunal has considered the totality of the above provisions
and precedents. There is no doubt that the Appellant has the duty to
produce relevant documents sought by the Respondent to support and
validate its objection that could then lead to the variation of additional
assessments. In the instant appeal, there is no evidence that the
appellant provided the relevant document to sufficiently support its
Objcetion in regard to the issues raised by the Respondent leading to
the issuance of the impugned objection decision.
56. Moreover, the appellant has not addressed the specific issues raised
by the respondent, that the total net amounts from the invoices
provided in support of the reconciliation did not match the figures in
the reconciliation table and ledgers. Additionally, the ledgers provided
only had totals for each category without a breakdown of the
transactions making up the grand totals.
57. The Respondent was obligated in law to explain and rebut these
issues raised by the Respondent using cogent explanations and
relevant documents to explain its position. This way, it would have
discharged its burden of proof, and the presumption of correctness of
the Respondent’s assessment would have disappeared, as was
explained in Commissioner of Domestic Taxes v. Trical and
Hard Limited (Tax Appeal E146 of 2020) [2022] KEHC 9927
(KLR) (Commercial and Tax) (8 July 2022) (Judgment) when it
stated as thus: -
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
n
Page 16 of
18

“―A presumption of correctness arises from the
Commissioner’s determination/assessment. The
presumption remains until the taxpayer produces
competent and relevant evidence to support his/her
position. When the taxpayer comes forward with such
evidence, the presumption vanishes, and the case
must be decided Upon the evidence presented.”
58. Accordingly, the Tribunal finds and holds that the Appellant in the
instant appeal has not discharged its burden of proof in accordance
with the relevant tax laws, and the evidence presented by the Tribunal
is not sufficient to make the presumption of correctness of the
Respondent’s assessment vanish or be set aside.
DISPOSITION
59. Based on the foregoing analysis, the Tribunal finds and holds that
the Appeal fails and shall proceed to make the following Orders: -
a. The Appeal be and is hereby dismissed.
b. Respondent’s Objection Decision dated 31st January, 2025 be
and is hereby upheld.
c. Each Party is to bear its costs.
60. It is so ordered.
DATED and DELIVERED at NAIROBI this …………27th...…. Day of ……
March…… 2026
Judgment TAT No. E316 of 2025 – Riana Properties Ltd -vs- Comm. Legal Services & Board
Coordinatio
